speaker: interviewer
text: On tape number 13. This is the interview with Michael Jackson. We are starting when he went back to Debrecen with his family.

speaker: interviewee
text: We went back. We took dad along to Debrecen. And my brother, Eliezer, contacted the doctors there. And the family had a family doctor. I don't remember him-- his name, rather. And he examined him. And he said that he would want him to, to, to go to the hospital. Now, you know, a Jew with a beard in, in, in, in, a Hungarian hospital, it wasn't so easy. But they took him into the hospital. And he stayed only for a week or ten days. And then they send him back. Yeah, I have to say that when my brother came to Debrecen in this two, it was two-room apartment, that my brother Eliezer had-- he, they, my, Eliezer's wife, Sari, she had a sister that lived in Debrecen. She was single, I think. So she took the children, send them over there to empty out the bed. So that's how, how, how, he was in there. And after a week's time, he came back from the hospital. And, of course, whatever medicines and the doctor would come every day. And it dragged on. It dragged on too much. to-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] I, I, I think, I think it was March 5 or March, March 6-- I know the, the, the, the Hebrew, Hebrew date. Oh. In meantime--

speaker: interviewee
text: The Hebrew date is the eighteenth month of Adar, which is, this year, it falls out in March, next, next month. What happened, I remember that before, before that date, my mother came. It was now my mother came also to, to Debrecen. And, and she said, she said, we need a ton of money.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in, in, in [INAUDIBLE] where my father's sister lived, we send out, she sent out her jewelry. She said I don't need any jewelry anymore. What do I need it, what do I need it for? And we needed a lot of money. The children are scattered all over. Let's go. She talked to me. She said, go and pick this up. And go sell it. I don't need, don't need it. So the, the, the task fell that I should go. So I traveled again. I was a little bit freer already, because I told her again I had this, this, this citizenship paper. It, it helped a little. I traveled again to Debrecen. And I was to travel and pick up all the jewelry that she had there, and my father's jewelry, and bring it to Debrecen. And we'll sell it over there. We'll make, we'll make money, because we, we, we need a lot of money. So I came to, to my aunt, Yuta, to [INAUDIBLE] And it had to be on a Wednesday. Wednesday night I remember, it was very cold in winter, is snow. And, and I came in there. And I told her. And I told her what I came for. And she said, fine. But why do you want to go back already tomorrow? She said, why don't you stay here already for over Shabbos, you know? And then, then you'll go back on Sunday. There's another day. She, she was-- you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: And stupid me, stupid me. I knew that my father is deadly sick there. And, and I should, I should take every opportunity to stay with him, to be close by. And here I am. It's Wednesday. I was going to go back on Thursday. She says, stay, stay over, over to over Saturday. Go back Sunday. And I agreed, agreed to stay, to stay over. And we stayed there. And you know, when you are, when you are young, you don't, you don't, you don't think rationally. And Saturday, Saturday, we get a telegram. I get a telegram-- come immediately. Come back.

speaker: interviewer
text: So and I, I, my brother, Eliezer, sent the telegram. And, and then I thought to myself, oh, my god. I'll be going back. And, and, and, my father will be, will be gone already.

speaker: interviewee
text: My aunt, she felt horrible that she kept me. But you know, in those days, even, even telegram-- it came on Saturday morning. We wouldn't travel on Saturday until it's after Saturday.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we waited till after, till after Shabbos, Saturday night. And it was winter time. We hired-- this was only 20 kilometers from Khust. We hired a guy with sleigh and a horse. And she took along-- it was very cold. She, I remember, she took along all the jewelry that she had-- our jewelry-- my aunt. And she took along blankets.

speaker: interviewee
text: We bundled up very good. And we made it to the train back to Khust. And we made the train. From Khust, it was about three, four hour train ride to go to Debrecen. Finally, we, we came. And fortunately, we came that Saturday night. And we found our father was, was alive. He fell into a coma before. And they thought it's, it's the end. But he was alive. So it was, it was in a way, we were, we were lucky. She so-- she was lamenting that he had, you had such a nice house and so much. My brother had a house. Each one had a house. And her father had a house. And here, you are all living in this one room. And she, I, I escorted her the next day to the train station. And she went, she went back. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: He regained consciousness. And, and he lived about a few weeks. I think-- I don't remember. I had it marked down. Should have it on the day in March. I'll look it up a little later. And one, that one day, my brother Tsvi and I-- we were smokers. Of course, we didn't smoke inside. We went out, we went out in the yard to have a smoke. And then, and then a few minutes later, my mother came out to the yard. And said, she said, children, children, she said-- you know, in Yiddish, she said, you can cry. You had a good father. And that, and that was, and that was the end of-- the we started to cry and run inside. And we. We saw him. He was, [? it was fine. ?] He was in great pain, in great pain. On one way, in one way, I think he was, he was lucky. He [? mattered ?] to know that his children were safe, more or less safe, and alive, and that we all were there, where he was buried in Debrecen. On the other hand, the, my mother was-- well, we'll talk about my mother later. I don't know when she died. At least I went back. I went back from Allentown to, to Debrecen. And it's, it's, my brother, Eliezer, and his family were rounded up in 1944, in April. And on, in March, he died in 1943 in March. In the [? matseyve, ?] you know, the tombstone, usually is put up eleven months later to the year. Was, was put up exactly-- he was able to put up a, a tombstone in, in a month before he, he was rounded up. And we never, we never, that, that, that ended that. And I think now, we'll, we will, we will go our different ways. My mother went back to [INAUDIBLE] as a widow. My brother Tvsi, my brother Tvsi went back to, to Mariapocs to his cousin, where he worked as a teacher there. I, I stayed, I stayed in, in Debrecen. And my brother, Moshe, went from there to [INAUDIBLE] He had some kind of a job. And that was, that was 1943. In May of 1943, one day I got up. And, and we see all placards all over, all over the city, that all Jewish males between the ages of 20, or 18 to 20, to 40, have to register in, in this and this place. And then those that were born on this year have to register in this place. And so that was, this was in 1943. I, I, I, my father was dead. My mother was a widow. And my, and we had no means anymore. And, and there was no, no, no way, no way that, that you can, you can get out of this hell anymore. So whatever, whatever go round like the destiny will be with, with the rest of us, I registered. And I came into, to a city called Karszow. And over there, they, they organized groups. This was a very infamous place. Because the, the, the head of that, of that organizing group was a guy by the name of [? Mikolac, ?] [? Mikolac, ?] a terrible anti-Semite, a zoological anti-Semite. Officially, it was supposed to be that you had to go through a physical examination. If you are not physically fit, then you are not good to, to, to go to war. But there was orders not to let down the lame, the, the, the, the deaf, the none, nobody. And I remember when I, when I-- there were a bunch of doctors standing with tables in the middle of a soccer field, and soccer field, or whatever field it was. It was fenced around. I don't remember. And when they came, I, I said, I'm healthy. No, you had to undress. You had to undress. And they counted up 200 or 220 and put you into a unit. And I fell into a unit that was called 104/108. And, and at the same time, my brother Tsvi that was in, in, in, in Mariapocs already at that time, he also couldn't, couldn't stay there anymore. He also had to get, to get in. And he, he got into a, to a-- his, his, I think, was [? 108/109. ?] And they organized those groups. And they-- we were sent out. We were sent out for training to a, a city near, you know, where Elie Wiesel was born in, in that city.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sighetu. Next to that city was a small place, small place, [INAUDIBLE] something. We stayed there for about two weeks. And then, and then they loaded us up. And they send us out behind the Danube River. They called it [INAUDIBLE], behind the Danube, into Yugoslavia. And over there, they were building a, a international airport. Now, they didn't have bulldozers like now. They had free slave laborers, Jews. And they gave them shovels and, and picks and, and wheelbarrows and for digging up mountains and filling in, in valleys. And so we stayed there in Yugoslavia. We stayed there for about-- in my, my group, where I, I was, we stayed there for about six weeks, six or eight weeks. And it so happened that my brother Tsvi was also in a, in another camp, but also sent over there. I, I found him. But he was in, he fell into a very bad, bad group. He couldn't get out. I, I, our group was-- it all depended on the commander of the group. If the commander was a half-decent person, he treated you half-decently. So I went to see him. And one day, we marched out. I saw him marching out. I was always usually-- and he too, because he was tall just like me. I, I was always in the front, in the front of the line-- the taller, smaller. You know how the military do. And, and so I, I always had a front view. I was seeing. And I saw, we saw each other. And we couldn't even get out to say goodbye to each other. And that was the last time-- sometimes in, in, in August of 19, 1943, that was the last time we saw each other. He was later shipped out deeper into, into Yugoslavia. He was sent to Bor That I found out later, that he was sent to Bor. And we were sent back to Hungary. And we were assigned between Budapest and, and [INAUDIBLE] . This is a city that the rail line was-- this was a one of the main lines that, that supplies to the, to the war front, was sent to. And the lines were weak. So they wanted to put in heavier lines. And it had to be done in  a very rushed. And so they, they took in some-- I don't remember how many thousand, maybe four, five, maybe a thousand of, of us boys to, to change the lines. And we thought, we thought that this was a very important job that has to be done. And already by-- this was 1943-- already by then, I, I think the, the war, the war started to tilt in favor of the Allies. And we were hoping that, that very soon, very soon, it, it will end. But sadly that it, it lasted for another, for almost, almost two years. And we worked there. I remember Yom Kippur, which, which usually comes out either the end of September or the beginning of October. We worked on that, on that railroad line. That was backbreaking labor. But we weren't hungry then. In hungry, our commander was a decent person. And, and he saw to it that we got enough food. We did very important work. And he said, he said that we have, if you, you want good work, you have to feed the people. So we did. And, and people that had relatives in September, October, people that had relatives were able to, to get a-- for the high holiday, for Yom Kippur-- a 24-hour, what they called in Hungarian, [HUNGARIAN] which meant that you can get away for 24 hours from your, from your your work.

speaker: interviewer
text: So since my brother Eliezer lived in Debrecen, and [INAUDIBLE], it was only a couple hours by train. So I figured I will go. And we will be together for the high holiday. So it turned out that, that the guy, that the sergeant, that was, that got the papers-- we had to have papers to get, to get away-- the officer signed the papers in time to, but he, he didn't want to give it to us.

speaker: interviewee
text: We finished up the work at 3:00. And there was a, a train that was going 3:30. So I figured, I'm going to take that train. But when it came the time, he, he refused to give it to us. So we missed, we missed the train. And after we missed the train, he gave it, he gave it to me. And then there was another train that was going around 5:00. And here again, I, I recount my biggest stupidity that I did. I got on the train 5:00, on the next train. And this was [INAUDIBLE] of Yom Kippur. And, and I was traveling. I, I would have, I would have come to Debrecen an hour or two late into the holiday. And, and, and in my stupidity, I, in one of the stations, the train stopped. I saw people going to the synagogues. I walked, I walked out in, in entirely a strange city. But I was never there, because I didn't want to desecrate the holiday of Yom Kippur.

speaker: interviewer
text: But what turned out was that the next day after I went into Debrecen, and, and I over, overstayed my, my, my 24 hours. And, and, and I took a chance on that. But that was the last time I saw this brother with his children and his wife. How much more would have been happier if I would have left and come in an hour late? When I look at it now, I see how stupid I was. But that's the way the upbringing that I had. And it was inside of me. I couldn't, I couldn't break, I couldn't break it. And but I, I managed to come back. And the next 24 hours, straight, straight to work.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that brings us to sometimes in October of 1943. We stayed there for another month at that work. And in October, '43-- in November, rather-- they packed us up. And we never knew where they're, where they're shipping us, where we are going. The military, you don't know. They put us on trains. And all we saw that we are traveling to the-- again to the Carpathian, to the, to the-- they are shipping us out to the front where the war is going on. But we stopped in, in-- for some reason, I don't know what the reason is-- we stopped in December. December 1, we stopped in [INAUDIBLE]. And we didn't do anything. We were taken off the trains and, and, and settled in in a place there. All we did was they would march us out every day. Why they, they didn't ship us straight out into, into the war front, I don't know. I think, I think at that winter of between '43 and '44, that was the winter when the Russians broke through the front in [INAUDIBLE] And [INAUDIBLE] was, was the line that the Hungarian military were holding. And they had a tremendous defeat in, in, in that, that time. So either that wasn't enough, enough rails-- I mean cars-- available, I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: But we stayed on for four weeks, almost to the end in December, almost to the end of December. In December, we finally got shipped out again into the Ukraine. Where are we now? And we got to the city of Stanislav.
